topic: Loading and visualizing geographic datasets using JSON and TSV formats in p5.js, including data parsing, type coercion issues, and non-linear scaling for better visual representation.

concepts.name: JSON vs TSV/CSV
concepts.explanation: The instructor explains that JSON is a structured object with keys and values, while TSV/CSV is tabular data organized in rows and columns, similar to a spreadsheet like Google Sheets or Excel.
concepts.why_it_matters: Understanding the structure of your data determines how you access its properties and which loading function to use.

concepts.name: loadTable()
concepts.explanation: A p5.js function used to load tabular data. It requires the filename, the file extension (like 'tsv'), and a parameter to indicate if there is a header row.
concepts.why_it_matters: It's the primary way to bring spreadsheet-style data into a creative coding sketch.

concepts.name: Type Coercion (String vs Number)
concepts.explanation: The instructor demonstrates how JavaScript can treat numbers as strings when they are loaded from text files, causing logical errors during comparisons (e.g., '9' being greater than '100').
concepts.why_it_matters: If you don't explicitly convert data to numbers, your mathematical operations and comparisons will fail or produce nonsensical results.

concepts.name: Non-linear Scaling (Math.sqrt)
concepts.explanation: Using `Math.sqrt()` on data like population to 'flatten the curve.' This prevents extreme outliers from making all other data points look identical.
concepts.why_it_matters: In datasets with huge discrepancies (like population), linear mapping makes most points look the same; non-linear scaling reveals more detail in the 'long tail'.

concepts.name: Array.sort() with custom compare function
concepts.explanation: Using `.sort()` with an arrow function to order data. The instructor uses this to draw smaller points first so that larger points are drawn on top and remain visible.
concepts.why_it_matters: The order in which you draw shapes determines what is layered on top of what; sorting helps manage visual occlusion.

code_examples.demonstrates: Loading a tab-separated text file with a header row.
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting to specify 'header' or using the wrong extension.

code_examples.demonstrates: Converting a p5.Table object into a standard 2D JavaScript array for easier manipulation.
code_examples.common_issue: Trying to use index notation (like `counties[0][1]`) directly on a Table object instead of an Array.

code_examples.demonstrates: Explicitly converting a string value from a data object into a number.
code_examples.common_issue: Comparing strings in loops, which leads to incorrect min/max calculations.

code_examples.demonstrates: Mapping a square-rooted population value to a color range.
code_examples.common_issue: Using raw population values in `map()` which causes most points to have the same color due to outliers.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Maximum call stack size exceeded error.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Likely an infinite loop or recursive function caused by improper data handling/loading.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Check the logic of loops and ensure callbacks are used correctly.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The min/max values are completely wrong (e.g., max is 99,000 but the actual data has millions).
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Type coercion: JavaScript is comparing strings alphabetically rather than numerically.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Wrap the data access in `Number()` or `parseFloat()`.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: p5.js error: 'you have used a p5.js reserved function "pop"'.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Using `pop` as a variable name when it is a reserved function in p5.js.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Rename the variable to something else (e.g., `population`).

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Large data points are covering up all the smaller ones.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Drawing order: larger elements are being drawn after smaller ones.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Sort the array so that smaller values are processed first in the loop.

key_moments.description: The instructor shows how to find real-world census data, emphasizing that data is a 'model of the world' and can change over time.
The instructor warns that if columns in a TSV file are not consistent, the data will get 'knocked out of sequence'.
The instructor explains why `Math.sqrt()` is useful for 'flattening the curve' of exponential data to make visualizations more legible.

student_questions: Why are my numbers not working in a comparison?
How do I load a text file or CSV into p5.js?
Why are all my dots the same color even though I used map()?
How do I make sure big shapes don't hide small ones?
What is the difference between JSON and a TSV file?
Why am I getting a 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' error?
How do I convert a string from my data into a number?
Why is '9' being treated as larger than '100'?
How do I sort my data before drawing it?
My dots are all in one spot, how do I map them to a map?
module: Module 4 – Poetry of The World, Oceans of Data
